cluster_name: Andy Burnham becomes UK's seventh prime minister in decade
tldr: At the headline layer the story is almost uniformly a political succession narrative — nearly all 25 country clusters frame Burnham as Britain's seventh prime minister in a decade of revolving-door chaos, with cabinet reshuffles and Starmer's exit as the secondary visual. US conservative outlets inject an ideological frame absent everywhere else: the Daily Wire declares 'World Eyes Rise Of Socialism' and Fox News World leads with Burnham as 'The Trump critic,' casting the transition as a geopolitical and ideological warning rather than a routine handover. The energy-bills VAT cut — Burnham's most concrete first-day policy — breaks through primarily in South Asian and African headlines (Pakistan, Nigeria) and in the UK's own Daily Mirror, while most European and wire-service outlets omit it from their titles entirely. Israel's Haaretz is the only outlet to lead with the foreign-policy dimension of the change ('tougher Israel stance expected'), a conspicuous silence given the stakes; France's Le Figaro uniquely foregrounds immigration and growth policy; and the Straits Times surfaces a telling juxtaposition — on the day Burnham pledged to empower the north of England, a northern city lost its direct train service to London.
event_summary: Andy Burnham became the United Kingdom's seventh prime minister in a decade on 21 July 2026, replacing Keir Starmer after meeting King Charles III at Buckingham Palace; Burnham immediately reshuffled the cabinet, removing several Starmer loyalists, and announced removal of VAT on household electricity bills as his first cost-of-living measure.

frames.sub_frame: 7th PM in decade; Starmer era ends; cabinet purged

evidence.quote: UK Prime Minister Andy Burnham unveils new government
After decade of chaos, UK's ruling Labour Party gambles
A big Cabinet reshuffle under the incoming PM

frames.sub_frame: VAT cut on electricity bills; £850m cost-of-living first move

evidence.quote: UK's Burnham targets lower energy bills in first move as PM
New UK government to cut tax on household electricity bills
Prime Minister scraps electricity bills VAT in £850 million
cuts energy bill tax for households

frames.sub_frame: 10-year plan; rewire Britain; social care pledge

evidence.quote: UK's new PM Andy Burnham promises a new 10-year plan
New PM Burnham vows 10-year plan for Britain
promises to use 'political capital' to tackle social care

frames.sub_frame: Trump critic label; tougher Israel stance

evidence.quote: How new PM Andy Burnham will change UK's international role
The Trump critic set to become the UK's next prime minister

single_outlet_findings.finding: The only headline in the entire corpus to invoke 'socialism' as an ideological frame — 'World Eyes Rise Of Socialism As New U.K. Prime Minister Takes Power' — treating Burnham's ascent as a global warning rather than a domestic handover. Every other outlet, including other US outlets, described his policies without ideological labelling at the title level.

One of two Straits Times headlines focuses on a northern English city losing direct train services to London on the very day Burnham pledged to decentralise power to the regions — the only outlet in the corpus to surface this ironic juxtaposition at the headline level.

One of two Globe and Mail headlines uses Larry the cat (Downing Street's long-serving resident) as the news hook — 'Larry the cat to welcome another PM to Downing Street' — the only outlet to adopt a whimsical human-interest frame, signalling lighter-touch coverage aimed at readers unfamiliar with UK politics.

bottom_line: Globally, headlines advertise Burnham's arrival almost exclusively as a POLITICAL succession story — the seventh PM in a decade — with his concrete first act (a £850m electricity-bills VAT cut) breaking through mainly in South Asian, African, and UK-domestic titles while most European and international outlets suppress it at homepage level. US right-wing media uniquely inject an ideological and geopolitical alarm ('socialism', 'Trump critic') absent from every other country's headlines; Haaretz alone foregrounds the Israel-policy shift. The 10-year reform agenda prominent in body coverage is largely absent from titles, surfacing only in France, the pan-Arab press, and Taiwan.
